text: The robotic camera provides a clear view of the duodenum, common bile duct, neck, and cystic region of the gallbladder, enhanced by the firefly mode and ICG dye to delineate the biliary anatomy.

The surgeon begins dissection at the neck of the gallbladder, incising the peritoneal fold and extending it anteriorly and posteriorly while staying close to the liver junction. The robotic arms facilitate precise dissection in various planes. The focus shifts to the dissection at Calot's triangle, where the cystic duct and cystic artery are freed from peritoneal attachments and fibro-fatty tissue.

The surgeon has created a plane between the cystic duct and cystic artery, with the cystic artery positioned superiorly and the cystic duct inferiorly. The Firefly mode is repeatedly used to accurately identify the anatomy. An aberrant vessel is visible entering segment four, which is preserved. The cystic duct is freed from surrounding fibro-fatty tissue to allow snug placement of clips, with ICG and Firefly mode aiding in defining the biliary anatomy for secure clipping.

The surgeon continues dissection to preserve the aberrant vessel. The cystic artery and cystic duct are clipped, with two clips applied distally and one proximally. Firefly mode is used to reconfirm the anatomy. Laparoscopic scissors are utilized to divide the cystic duct and artery, ensuring the aberrant vessel is carefully protected.

The surgeon frees the gallbladder from its attachments at the liver bed, using a camera flip technique for enhanced visualization. The aberrant vessel is completely dissected out and preserved. The gallbladder is carefully dissected away, encountering loose areolar tissue and some inflammation due to acute calculus cholecystitis.
